sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Eye injuries refer to any harm or trauma affecting the eye or its surrounding structures, including the eyelids, eye socket, and nerves. These can vary in severity from minor surface abrasions and corneal scratches to serious penetrating injuries and chemical burns.
Prompt recognition and appropriate management are essential in all cases to preserve vision and prevent serious complications, including permanent vision loss.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Blunt Trauma: Caused by a direct blow to the eye (e.g., sports injuries or car accidents).
This can result in black eyes, orbital fractures, or damage to internal eye structures like the retina
- Penetrating or Sharp Injuries: Occur when a sharp object punctures or cuts the eye, potentially causing serious damage
- Chemical Burns: Result from exposure to harmful substances, such as cleaning products or industrial chemicals, which can irritate or damage the eye tissues
- Foreign Objects: Small particles like dust, sand, or metal shavings can enter the eye and cause irritation, scratches, or infections
- Thermal or Radiation Injuries: Caused by heat (e.g., explosions, fireworks) or exposure to harmful radiation (e.g., UV rays from the sun or welding arcs)
- Infections: Sometimes, bacterial, viral, or fungal infections resulting from an injury can lead to complications like conjunctivitis or keratitis

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Significant eye injury may be present despite a relatively benign appearance
- If eyelid oedema makes opening the lids difficult, attempt only gentle assessment and document findings
- Avoid manipulating the globe or retracting lids forcefully
- With penetrating injury or suspected globe rupture, manipulation may cause extrusion of ocular contents
- When irrigating one eye, keep the affected side dependent where possible to avoid contaminating the other eye
- For chemical injury, immediate copious irrigation takes priority and should begin as early as possible
- Depending on the injury, urgent ophthalmic or surgical care may be required

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration where indicated
- Reassure the patient, keep them at rest, and discourage eye movement
- Manage catastrophic bleeding as required
- Do not manipulate the injured eye

**For penetrating eye injury or suspected globe rupture:**
- Do not remove any penetrating foreign body from the globe
- If tolerated, place a rigid protective eye shield over the affected eye; do not pad the eye

**For chemical burns:**
- Start immediate irrigation with water, saline, or other suitable irrigant; if Diphoterine is immediately available it may be used, but irrigation should not be delayed
- Continue irrigation during transport where required
- If litmus paper is available, pH testing may help guide ongoing irrigation

**For lid lacerations:**
- If bleeding control is required, apply pressure around the eye while avoiding pressure on the globe

- Position the patient comfortably; slight head elevation may be helpful if tolerated
- Document vision, pupil findings, eye movements, and major symptoms where assessment is possible
- Secondary / CNS Survey
- Fluorescein assessment should not be used if globe rupture is suspected
- Clinicians should have a low threshold for consultation with Poisons Information, RFDS, or ophthalmology support where relevant

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access if indicated
- Consider antiemetic if nausea or vomiting is present
- Consider analgesia
- Monitor the patient regularly, recording observations every 10 minutes where appropriate
- Escalate and transport urgently for chemical injury, penetrating injury, suspected globe rupture, significant visual loss, or time-critical trauma

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Routine padding is no longer recommended. If padding is used, it must not place pressure on the globe. Do not pad an eye with a penetrating injury
- Blunt trauma examples, eye lid injury, orbital blow-out fracture, corneal abrasion, anterior hyphema, traumatic mydriasis, lens dislocation, vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment, traumatic iritis, ruptured globe
